channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Average U.S. Gasoline Price Falls Below $4 for First Time in Months
item.description: Drivers are enjoying some relief at the pump after the United States and Iran signed an agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gasoline prices rose sharply after the start of U.S.-led war with Iran.

item.title: Before Making a Deal, Trump Demanded Iran’s Surrender. He Got a Surprise.
item.description: While the Iranians suffered substantial losses in the war, they emerged from a confrontation with the world’s most powerful military having proved they can use economic chaos as a weapon.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at a Group of 7 summit in France on Wednesday. The president has argued he has the upper hand with an agreement that leaves negotiations over Iran’s nuclear program for a later date.

item.title: Trump’s Deal With Iran Opens New Rifts in G.O.P.
item.description: Some in the president’s party were skeptical about whether the agreement he reached included adequate concessions from Iranian officials.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner, Robert Jimison and Tim Balk
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump on Wednesday at the Group of 7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France. Some Republican leaders have balked at the terms of Mr. Trump’s preliminary arrangement to end the war in Iran.

item.title: How the U.S.-Iran Deal Came Down to the Wire
item.description: The agreement, which punted many of the toughest issues to future negotiations, came after a last-minute scramble.
item.dc:creator: Adam Rasgon, Ronen Bergman, Farnaz Fassihi, David E. Sanger, Elian Peltier and Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump at the U.F.C. fight at the White House on Sunday, on the evening of the deal’s announcement.

item.title: Trump Defends Deal to End the War With Iran as Details Emerge
item.description: President Trump lashed out at critics who say the agreement achieves less than the one President Barack Obama signed in 2015, and he threatened to bomb Iran again if it violated the deal.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green, Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Farnaz Fassihi and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump and other U.S. officials at a news conference amid the Group of 7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, on Wednesday.

item.title: Bill Pulte, a Bulldog for Trump, Prepares to Take Reins at Spy Agency
item.description: Democrats and some Republicans excoriated President Trump’s pick for a top national security job. The president pushed ahead anyway.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Dustin Volz
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:description: Bill Pulte has used his current post as a top federal housing official to help with President Trump’s campaign of retribution against his perceived enemies.

item.title: Trump Feuds With Thune and G.O.P., Stoking Election-Year Rift
item.description: The president upended the majority leader’s plans by yanking his intelligence nominee from a confirmation hearing and insisting on an end to the filibuster.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:description: Senator John Thune, Republican of South Dakota and the majority leader, has shown more willingness in recent days to push back against President Trump no matter the fallout.

item.title: F.T.C. Sues Group That Advises on Transgender Medical Treatments
item.description: The group, World Professional Association for Transgender Health, offers guidelines on medical treatment for transgender patients.
item.dc:creator: Amy Harmon, Christina Jewett and David McCabe
item.category: World Professional Association for Transgender Health
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Families with transgender minors have struggled to find consistent medical treatments as state laws have shifted in recent years.

item.title: Trump Rescinds Endorsement of Right-Wing Pastor After Texting Scandal
item.description: The pastor, Jackson Lahmeyer, dropped out of the race for a House seat in Oklahoma as President Trump backed Mr. Lahmeyer’s Republican rival in a runoff election.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Michael Noble Jr. for The New York Times; Tedford for U.S. Congress
item.media:description: Jackson Lahmeyer, left, and Mark Tedford had advanced to a Republican primary runoff election on Tuesday, but Mr. Lahmeyer ended his bid on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Administration Shares Terms of Iran Deal Including Oil Export Waivers
item.description: The document leaves complicated issues like Iran’s nuclear program still to be decided and a 60-day window to determine them.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump and other leaders of the Group of 7 countries in France on Wednesday. A senior official read the U.S. agreement with Iran to reporters as Mr. Trump held a news conference.

item.title: Susan Collins Laments Roe’s Fall but Doesn’t ‘Regret’ Confirming Kavanaugh
item.description: Ms. Collins, a Republican in Maine facing a tough re-election battle, defended her vote to confirm Justice Brett M. Kavanaugh as Democrats look to capitalize on it politically.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Susan Collins of Maine is one of only two Republican senators who support abortion rights.

item.title: See How the First Fed Statement Under Warsh Evolved
item.description: Interest rates didn’t change, but the language in Fed’s policy statement, which it released alongside its rate decision today, certainly did.
item.dc:creator: Christine Zhang

item.title: Trump’s Threats Show His Complicated History With a Surveillance Law
item.description: The administration argues that FISA Section 702 must be renewed to preserve national security, but the president himself has threatened to veto it.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:description: President Trump has for years veered sharply between condemning the law and embracing it as a necessary intelligence tool that helps prevent terrorism, cyberattacks and other threats.

item.title: Can Trump’s Man in Georgia Take Down Jon Ossoff?
item.description: Representative Mike Collins’s victory in a hard-fought Republican primary means that a big Senate matchup is now set.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Kendrick Brinson for The New York Times; Colin Hubbard/Associated Press
item.media:description: Senator Jon Ossoff of Georgia, left, and his Republican challenger, Representative Mike Collins.

item.title: Georgia Republicans Shelve Redistricting Amid Mounting Protests
item.description: The leaders of the Georgia legislature pulled the plug on gerrymandering away U.S. House seats held by Black Democrats just hours before a special session was to begin.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas, Sean Keenan and Alessandro Marazzi Sassoon
item.media:description: Protesters gathered at the Georgia state capitol in Atlanta Wednesday, determined to stop the legislature from redistricting the state.

item.title: U.S.-Iran Agreement Includes Strait of Hormuz, Lebanon and More: See Full Text of the Deal
item.description: New York Times reporters annotated the agreement, which a senior official disclosed on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament

item.title: Under Trump, National Intelligence Agency Has Broadened Focus on Elections
item.description: Bill Pulte, who has shown that he has a keen sense of what the president wants and a desire to please him, is poised to take over the job from Tulsi Gabbard on an acting basis.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tulsi Gabbard, the director of national intelligence, at the Fulton County elections office in January.

item.title: Conor McGregor Defends Treatment That Included Banned Drugs
item.description: The Ultimate Fighting Championship star said his priority was walking again after shattering his leg, and he confirmed elements of a New York Times article about how he came to use banned substances.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:description: Conor McGregor attending an N.B.A. game in Salt Lake City last year.

item.title: Elon Musk’s Next Move May Be a Mega-Merger of SpaceX and Tesla
item.description: Some shareholders might object, but there is little they could do, legal experts say.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Tesla has sold car and batteries to SpaceX, and the two companies are jointly working on chips and software.

item.title: Choosing How to Kill Death Row Inmates
item.description: In a rare ruling for a condemned prisoner, the justices would not let Alabama use a contested method of execution.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:description: An execution chamber in Alabama. Last week, the Supreme Court refused to allow the state to carry out an execution by nitrogen gas.

item.title: Intelligence Officials Have Welcomed Pulte and Then Clayton
item.description: The two, whom President Trump nominated as acting director and director of national intelligence, have both prepared to take over the office.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:description: Bill Pulte, currently the top federal housing official, at the Capitol last year.

item.title: California’s Vote Count: How Slowness Invites Suspicion Even When It’s Not Sketchy
item.description: California’s crawling pace after election night can make normal statistical shifts seem like fraud.
item.dc:creator: Emily Badger and Alicia Parlapiano

item.title: Anthropic Employees Accuse Trump Administration of Targeting Them
item.description: Workers at the artificial intelligence company have been puzzled and increasingly concerned by the administration’s move to limit their latest A.I. models.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel, Julian E. Barnes and Dustin Volz
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Anthropic’s headquarters in San Francisco, where some employees are wondering if they are being picked on by President Trump.

item.title: Trump Angrily Defends Iran Deal and Reveals, Again, His Obsession With Obama
item.description: President Trump denied that the United States would be part of a $300 billion rebuilding fund for Iran and argued that his agreement was better than the one Barack Obama struck in 2015.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at the Group of 7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France, on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump Pulls Back Intelligence Pick to Pressure Congress on Elections Bill
item.description: President Trump named Jay Clayton, the U.S. attorney in Manhattan, after a bipartisan backlash to his earlier choice of Bill Pulte. The move all but assures that Mr. Pulte will hold the job, at least for a while.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold and Dustin Volz
item.media:description: Senate leaders had been hoping that Jay Clayton could be quickly confirmed.

item.title: What to Know About Redistricting Efforts Underway in Georgia
item.description: Georgia is the latest Southern state where lawmakers have taken up redrawing congressional maps after the Supreme Court’s voting rights ruling.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Georgia Republicans are meeting to draw new lines for 2028. The governor said it was too late to redistrict for this year because early voting in the primary was already underway.

item.title: This Senator Is an Internet Sensation. Is He Running for President?
item.description: The 39-year-old senator has become an internet sensation for Democrats seeking a 2028 contender. He says he’s focused on winning a second term in November.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein and Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Kendrick Brinson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jon Ossoff of Georgia is the only Senate Democrat seeking re-election in a state President Trump won in 2024.

item.title: Who Will Get Spirit Airlines’ Coveted Flights at LaGuardia?
item.description: A bankruptcy court has started the process to sell the airline’s right to fly to and from the New York City airport, but finding a buyer could be hard.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: Port Authority of New York and New Jersey
item.media:credit: DeSean McClinton-Holland for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Spirit Airlines guest services counter at LaGuardia Airport.
